audienceProblem: How do I choose the simplest AI-agent architecture that can reliably complete the task?
centralThesis: Start with the simplest system that can solve the task, and add workflow or agent complexity only when measured results improve.

reusableArtifact.name: Agent Architecture Decision Ladder
steps: Test one augmented model call first
Use a workflow when the path can be predefined
Use orchestrator-workers when subtasks are discoverable only at runtime
Use an autonomous agent only when the whole path depends on environmental feedback
Keep the added layer only if completion rate, cost, latency, or human intervention measurably improves

P0: The difference between workflows and agents
The augmented LLM as the default starting point
P1: Five workflow patterns and the uncertainty each resolves
When autonomy is justified and how environmental feedback controls it
P2: Representative support, coding, refund, translation, and incident-response examples
Framework abstraction, observability, stopping conditions, permissions, and reversibility boundaries
P3: Appendix implementation details that do not change the decision ladder

chapters.promise: Expose why complex agent architecture is often the wrong starting point
Separate workflows from agents and tie complexity to measurable need
Start with one model plus retrieval, tools, and memory
Match five controlled patterns to five kinds of uncertainty
Use autonomy only with real feedback, boundaries, and stopping conditions
Make correct tool use easier and common mistakes harder
Apply a reusable architecture decision ladder
Remember the ladder and the measurable upgrade rule

roles.engineer: Frames the task, chooses boundaries, checks evidence, and approves risky actions
roles.tinyAgent: Retrieves facts, uses tools, acts, reads environmental feedback, stores state, and returns results

sharedVisualSemantics: Retrieval, tools, and memory around one model
Clear tool interface versus avoidable format friction
Architecture ladder validated by measurable outcomes
languagePolicy: English and Chinese share thesis, priorities, chapter order, fact boundaries, character tasks, and the reusable artifact. Wording and timing are authored independently.
